title: E[X | X > 0] for N(0,1)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-03-calculus-linear-algebra.md

setup.narrative: X is a standard normal random variable, X ~ N(0, 1). The probability density function is f(x) = (1/sqrt(2*pi)) * e^(-x^2/2).
rules: E[X | X > 0] = (integral from 0 to infinity of x * f(x) dx) / P(X > 0).
P(X > 0) = 1/2 by the symmetry of the standard normal.
setup.question: What is E[X | X > 0]?

learningObjectives: Set up the conditional expectation as a ratio of two integrals.
Use u-substitution u = -x^2/2 to evaluate the numerator integral.
Divide by the tail probability to get the conditional expectation.

stages.label: Set up the conditional expectation
stages.prompt: How do you compute E[X | X > 0]?

options.text: (integral from 0 to infinity of x * f(x) dx) / P(X > 0).
integral from 0 to infinity of x * f(x) dx, no division needed.

stages.explanation: Conditional expectation = E[X * indicator(X > 0)] / P(X > 0). The numerator integrates x against the joint of (X, X > 0).

stages.label: Choose your technique for the numerator
stages.prompt: Compute integral from 0 to infinity of x * (1/sqrt(2*pi)) * e^(-x^2/2) dx. What's the right substitution?

options.text: u = -x^2/2,  du = -x dx.  The x dx in the integrand becomes -du, simplifying everything.

stages.explanation: u-substitution. The integrand has the form (something) * x dx, so set u = (something exponent) and du captures the x dx factor.

stages.label: Evaluate the numerator
stages.prompt: After substitution u = -x^2/2, the integral becomes (1/sqrt(2*pi)) * integral from 0 to -infinity of e^u * (-du) = (1/sqrt(2*pi)) * [-e^(-x^2/2)] from 0 to infinity. What does this evaluate to?
stages.explanation: At x = infinity, e^(-x^2/2) -> 0. At x = 0, e^0 = 1. So [-e^(-x^2/2)] from 0 to infinity = 0 - (-1) = 1. Multiply by 1/sqrt(2*pi) to get 1/sqrt(2*pi).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why is E[X | X > 0] not simply 0? (X has mean 0 unconditionally.)
Compute E[X | X > a] for general a > 0. (Use the same substitution.)
Compute E[X^2 | X > 0]. Does the same trick work?
